docs.title: Learning Resources
docs.text:
• Tour of Go
  • Effective Go
  • Go by Example
  • Standard library

docs.title: Arrays
docs.text:
The length of an array is fixed and part of its type. 

// Declaration
var array [n]type
// Length defined
var b = [3]int{1, 2, 3}
// Length inferred
var c = [...]int{1, 2, 3, 4}

docs.title: Constants
docs.text:
Declaration: const name type = expression

const greeting string = "Hello World"

docs.title: Untyped Constants
docs.text:
Constants can be untyped which is useful for number. The constant will be implicitly converted to the required type.

Examples from package math: 

const (
E        = 2.71828182845904523536028747135266249775724709369995957496696763
Pi       = 3.14159265358979323846264338327950288419716939937510582097494459
MaxInt8  = 1<<7 - 1 // 127
MinInt32 = -1 << 31 // -2147483648
)

docs.title: Enums
docs.text:
Go has no enumerations but they can be mimicked by using constants. The value for the keyword iota is incremented by one after each line. 

const (
Summer int = iota // 0
Autumn            // 1
Winter            // 2
Spring            // 3
)
type Direction int
const (
North Direction = iota // 0
East                   // 1
South                  // 2
West                   // 3
)

docs.title: Type conversions
docs.text:
Type conversions T(x) must be explicit and different types need to be compatible.

var f float64 = 1.2
// fraction is discarded
var i int = int(f)
var s string = "Hello World"
var r []rune = []rune(s)
var b []byte = []byte(s)

docs.title: Package strconv
docs.text:
Conversions to and from string representations of basic data types. 

// Integer To ASCII
s := strconv.Itoa(10)
// Equivalent to ParseInt(s, 10, 0)
i, err := strconv.Atoi("10")

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
The type comes after the variable name. 

func add(x int, y int) int {
return x + y
}
// same type of parameters
func sub(x, y int) int {
return x - y
}
// named return value
func multiply(x, y int) (z int) {
z = x * y
return
}
// multiple return values
func divide(x, y int) (int, int) {
return x / y, x % y
}

docs.title: Defer
docs.text:
Defer statements delay the execution until the function that contains the defer keyword has finished. This is similar to a finally block in other languages. 

func main() {
defer fmt.Print("world")
fmt.Print("hello ")
// Output: hello world
}
// Multiple defer statements
func main() {   defer fmt.Print("done")
for i := 1; i <= 3; i++ {
defer fmt.Printf("%v ", i)
}
// Output: 3 2 1 done
}
// Cleanup resources
func main() {   file, err := os.Create("filename")
if err != nil {
panic(err)
}
defer file.Close()
}

docs.title: Variadic
docs.text:
A variadic function accepts any number of values as a single argument. The type of the last parameter is preceded by an ellipsis (...). 

func sum(nums ...int) int {
var total = 0
for num := range nums {
total += num
}
return total
}

docs.title: Default
docs.text: Unlike many other languages Go does not support optional/default values.

docs.title: Hello World
docs.text:
Program execution begins by initializing the main package and then invoking the function main.

package main
import "fmt"
func main() {
fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
}

docs.title: If statements
docs.text:
There is no ternary operator in Go.

if condition1 {
// condition1 is true
} else if condition2 {
// condition2 is true
} else {
// both conditions are false
}

docs.title: If with a short statement
docs.text:
Variables declared at the start are available inside all branches.

if assignment; condition {
// code to execute
}

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if number, err := strconv.Atoi("10"); err == nil {
sum += number
}

docs.title: Interfaces
docs.text:
An interface contains a collection of method signatures. Interfaces are implemented implicitly. There is no implements keyword. 

// Declaration
type name interface{}
type Shape interface {
Area() float64
}
// Struct for implementation
type rectangle struct {
width, height float64
}
func (r rectangle) Area() float64 {
return r.Width * r.Height
}

docs.title: The empty interface
docs.text: An empty interface (interface{} or any with Go 1.18+) is implemented by all types.

docs.title: Type assertions
docs.text:
Access the underlying concrete type of an interface with type assertion. 

var i interface{} = "text"
// panic if type is wrong
var s string = i.(string)
// check type
s, ok := i.(string)

docs.title: Type switches
docs.text:
Instead of the specific type, the keyword type is used. 

var i interface{} = 1
switch v := i.(type) {
case int:
// type of v = int
case string:
// type of v = string
default:
// type of v = same type as i
}

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text:
Language Specification: https://go.dev/ref/spec#Keywords 

break        default      func         interface    select
case         defer        go           map          struct
chan         else         goto         package      switch
const        fallthrough  if           range        type
continue     for          import       return       var

docs.title: Loops
docs.text:
The for loop is the only loop available in Go.

for initialization; condition; post {
// code to execute
}
for condition {
// "while" loop
}
for {
// infinite loop
}
for index, value := range elements {
// foreach loop
}

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
for i := 0; i < 10; i++ {
// break or continue
}
dictionary := make(map[string]string)
for key, value := range dictionary {
// skip key or value with '_'
}

docs.title: Maps
docs.text:
A map stores a collection of key/value pairs. In other languages a map is called a hash table or dictionary. The key type in a map (map[keyType]valueType) must be a comparable type. 

// Create a map
var m = make(map[string]string)
// Insert or update
m["key"] = "value"
// Get element or zero value
var value = m["key"]
// Contains key
v, ok := m["key"]
// Delete
delete(m, "key")
// Map Literal
var table = map[int]string{
1: "A",
2: "B",
3: "C",
}

docs.title: Nil map
docs.text: Writing to a nil map will panic. Initialize a map with the built in make function.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
Methods are similar to functions but they operate on instances of a specific type (receiver). There is no this orself keyword in Go but it is a convention to use the first letter of the receiver.

type rectangle struct {
width, height float64
}
func (r rectangle) Area() float64 {
return r.Width * r.Height
}

docs.title: Value and pointer receivers
docs.text: A value receiver (t T) uses a copy of the original value. To modify the original value a pointer receiver (t *T) is needed. For consistency, all methods on a type should use the same receiver type, even if not needed.

docs.title: Named Types
docs.text:
Named types can be used to differ between multiple incompatible types with the same underlying type. 

// Declaration
type name underlyingType
// Example
type Celsius float64
type Fahrenheit float64

docs.title: Pointers
docs.text:
A pointer variable stores the memory address of a value. There is no pointer arithmetic in Go. 

// Declaration
var ptr *type
// Zero value is nil
var p *int
// Address-of operator (&)
var x = 10
p = &x
// Dereference operator (*)
fmt.Println(*p) // 10
*p = 20
fmt.Println(*p) // 20
fmt.Println(x)  // 20

docs.title: Slices
docs.text:
A slice is like a reference to an array. The length of a slice can change by re-slicing or appending items. 

// Create a slice from an array
var array = [5]int{0, 1, 2, 3, 4}
// The "end" element is excluded
var slice = array[start:end]
// Slice literal
var hello = []byte{'h', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o'}
// Make a slice with zero values
var name = make([]type, length, capacity)

docs.title: Appending to a slice
docs.text:
The built-in append function also works on nil slices.

var slice []int
slice = append(slice, 1)
var slice2 = []int{2, 3}
// ... unpacks the second slice
slice = append(slice, slice2...)

docs.title: Empty slice
docs.text: Check for an empty slice with len(s) == 0 and not s == nil because for example s := []int{} is not nil.

docs.title: Structs
docs.text:
A struct can group data with different types. 

// Declaration
type name struct{}
// Named fields in a struct
type Point struct {
X int
Y int
}
// New struct
var p1 = Point{1, 2}
var p2 = Point{X: 1, Y: 2}
// Zero values
var p3 Point = Point{}

Fields can be accessed with a struct pointer without explicit dereference. Example: Write p.X instead of (*p).X.

docs.title: Switch statement
docs.text:
In Go a break statement is added implicitly at the end of each case. The fallthrough keyword can be used to also execute the next case. 

// Multiple values
switch month {
case 1, 2, 3:
fmt.Println("Spring")
case 4, 5, 6:
fmt.Println("Summer")
case 7, 8, 9:
fmt.Println("Autumn")
case 10, 11, 12:
fmt.Println("Winter")
}
// No condition
switch {
case hour < 12:
fmt.Println("AM")
case hour < 24:
fmt.Println("PM")
default:
fmt.Println("Invalid")
}

docs.title: Basic data types
docs.text:
bool
string
int  int8  int16  int32  int64
uint uint8 uint16 uint32 uint64 uintptr
byte // alias for uint8
rune // alias for int32 (Unicode code point)
float32 float64
complex64 complex128

docs.title: Zero values
docs.text:
Variables declared without an initial value are set to their zero values:

  • 0 for numbers
  • "" for strings
  • false for booleans

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
The variable's type can be inferred from the assigned value.

Declaration: var name type = expression 

var s = ""
var s string
var s string = ""

Short declarations: name := expression Not for package-level variables, only inside a function!

s := ""

docs.title: Visibility
docs.text:
If a name begins with an upper-case letter, it is exported (PascalCase). Any unexported (camelCase) names are not visible or accessible from outside the package.

There are no access modifiers such as public or private.

docs.title: Internal packages
docs.text:
Put code which should not be used outside the program in a directory named internal.

Go 1.4 release notes: https://golang.org/doc/go1.4#internalpackages

docs.title: Package fmt
docs.text:
Package fmt implements formatted I/O with functions analogous to C's printf and scanf. The format 'verbs' are derived from C's but are simpler.

Useful Functions: 

Errorf(format string, a ...any) error
Fprint(w io.Writer, a ...any) (n int, err error)
Printf(format string, a ...any) (n int, err error)
Sprintf(format string, a ...any) string

Common format verbs: 

%b      integer (base 2)
%c      character (Unicode code point)
%d      integer (base 10)
%o      integer (base 8)
%q      quoted string "abc" or rune 'c'
%s      string
%T      type of any value
%v      the value in a default format
%+v     structs: adds field names
%%      literal percent sign (no operand)

Decimal numbers: 

%f      precision 6 (default)
%.f     precision 0
%.2f    precision 2

docs.title: Package slices
docs.text:
Released in Go 1.21 Package slices defines various functions useful with slices of any type.

Useful Functions: 

Contains[S ~[]E, E comparable](s S, v E) bool
Index[S ~[]E, E comparable](s S, v E) int

Compare[S ~[]E, E cmp.Ordered](s1, s2 S) int
Equal[S ~[]E, E comparable](s1, s2 S) bool

Min[S ~[]E, E cmp.Ordered](x S) E
Max[S ~[]E, E cmp.Ordered](x S) E

Reverse[S ~[]E, E any](s S)
Sort[S ~[]E, E cmp.Ordered](x S)

docs.title: Package strconv
docs.text:
Package strconv implements conversions to and from string representations of basic data types.

Useful Functions: 

Atoi(s string) (int, error)
Itoa(i int) string
ParseInt(s string, base int, bitSize int) (i int64, err error)

docs.title: Package strings
docs.text:
Package strings implements simple functions to manipulate UTF-8 encoded strings. For information about UTF-8 strings in Go, see https://blog.golang.org/strings.

Useful Functions: 

Contains(s, substr string) bool
HasPrefix(s, prefix string) bool
HasSuffix(s, suffix string) bool
Index(s, substr string) int
Join(elems []string, sep string) string
Repeat(s string, count int) string
ReplaceAll(s, old, new string) string
Split(s, sep string) []string
ToLower(s string) string
ToUpper(s string) string
TrimSpace(s string) string

Useful Types: 

Builder
Replacer

docs.title: Package unicode
docs.text:
Package unicode provides data and functions to test some properties of Unicode code points.

Useful Functions: 

ToLower(r rune) rune
ToUpper(r rune) rune
IsLetter(r rune) bool
IsNumber(r rune) bool
IsDigit(r rune) bool
